Simulation study summary

Dense three-state Markov-chain i-block CP experiments. This report uses max_permutations = 100.

Run setting. State space {1,2,3}; dense adjacency; n_sim = 500; T = 500; horizons h = 1, 2, 3; alpha = 0.05, 0.10, ..., 0.50; randomized_ties = False. The three methods are original i-block CP, permutation-count D!-weighted auxiliary CP, and i-block-count D-weighted auxiliary CP. The cap max_permutations = 100 only limits how many block permutations are evaluated when estimating each p-value; it does not change D or the theoretical D! group size used in the weights.

Main conclusions

  • Sim 1: In the smooth dense chain, original CP is conservative and has the largest sets. D-weighted CP reduces set size relative to original while keeping coverage mostly safe. D!-weighted CP gives smaller sets but shows more undercoverage risk.
  • Sim 2: In the deterministic two-cycle, D-weighted CP is the clearest winner: it gives the correct singleton set, while original CP is wider and D!-weighted CP can return empty sets.
  • Sim 3: In the deterministic three-cycle, original CP is already very efficient. D-weighted CP matches this behavior at standard alpha levels. D!-weighted CP again shows instability for longer horizons.
  • Overall: D-weighting should not be described as uniformly better than original i-block CP. The stronger statement is that it is a more stable auxiliary weighting than D!, and it can improve efficiency in structured histories without the empty-set behavior seen under D! weighting.

Sim 1: Smooth dense three-state chain

  • This is the baseline dense Markov case. The transition matrix is not deterministic, and all three states occur with nontrivial probability.
  • Original i-block CP is conservative and has the largest sets.
  • D!-weighted auxiliary CP gives the smallest sets, but has some undercoverage at longer horizons.
  • D-weighted auxiliary CP is a middle ground: smaller sets than original, with coverage mostly at or above the target level.

Sim 2: Deterministic two-cycle

  • The chain alternates between states 1 and 2. The true future is essentially determined by the last observed state.
  • Original i-block CP covers the truth, but its sets are wider than necessary.
  • D-weighted auxiliary CP gives the correct singleton set at the usual alpha levels in this run.
  • D!-weighted auxiliary CP can be too aggressive here and may return empty sets for some horizons.

Sim 3: Deterministic three-cycle

  • The chain cycles 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 1. This is also essentially deterministic.
  • Original i-block CP already recovers the correct singleton set in this case.
  • D-weighted auxiliary CP matches the good behavior of original CP at alpha = 0.20, though the reliability curve has a sharp threshold because the data are deterministic.
  • D!-weighted auxiliary CP again shows instability for longer horizons, including empty sets at alpha = 0.20.
